description: Seed clip library for Lahjat Classic mode (Weekend 1 prototype). Each clip references a YouTube video + start timestamp and an 'answer' tying back to dialect-cities.json (city, cluster, macro_group, coordinates). Clips with broad/ambiguous source labels are flagged with verification_status and alternate_acceptable_clusters so scoring can credit a near-miss at the macro_group level even if the exact cluster guess differs.

clips.notes: Generic 'Egyptian Arabic' defaults to Cairene/Delta (the prestige/media norm). If the speaker has Saidi features (e.g. characteristic intonation, lexical items from Upper Egypt), reassign cluster to 'saidi'.
clips.reveal_draft: This is Cairene Egyptian Arabic, the dialect most non-Arabs picture first thanks to Egypt's film and broadcast industry. Listen for /q/ realized as a glottal stop (qalb "heart" becomes more like "alb"), jim pronounced as a hard /g/ rather than /j/, and the wraparound negation ma-...-sh. Everyday markers like dilwa'ti ("now") and izzayyak ("how are you") are classic Cairene tells.

clips.notes: Generic 'Iraqi Arabic' is the broadest label in this set — Iraqi dialects split sharply along the gilit/qeltu line (Baghdadi vs Moslawi) plus Southern Iraqi and Anbari Bedouin varieties. Defaulting to Baghdadi (gilit) as the unmarked case, but this needs a listen: qaf as /g/ + gilit verb forms = Baghdadi; qeltu forms + closer to Aleppine = Moslawi.
clips.reveal_draft: Tagged here as Baghdadi (gilit) Iraqi Arabic, though Iraq's dialect map is unusually split and this is one to double-check by ear. Gilit dialects (Baghdad and much of the south) shift /q/ to /g/ (qalb -> galb) and take their name from the gilit verb pattern itself. If you instead hear qeltu-style forms with /q/ retained as a uvular stop, this clip is linguistically closer to Mosul than to Baghdad, a genuinely different dialect group despite both being labeled "Iraqi."

clips.notes: Cluster assignment (moroccan) is solid given the label, but the specific city (Casablanca) is a placeholder representative for the cluster, not a verified geolocation of the speaker. Fine for cluster-level scoring; downgrade city-level scoring confidence or omit a 'correct city' bonus for this clip unless the speaker's actual hometown is known.
clips.reveal_draft: Moroccan Darija stands out for heavy vowel reduction that can compress whole words down to consonant clusters, such as ktbt for "I wrote." Other markers include the question particle wash, the intensifier bezzaf ("a lot"), and a noticeably faster rhythm than Levantine or Gulf speech. Darija also carries a heavier layer of French and Amazigh (Berber) vocabulary than dialects further east.

clips.notes: Same caveat as clip03 — 'Libyan Arabic' confidently maps to the libyan cluster, but Tripoli (western) vs Benghazi (eastern, closer to Egyptian features) vs Fezzan are quite different at the city level. Verify before using as a city-level answer.
clips.reveal_draft: Libyan Arabic sits at the eastern edge of the Maghrebi zone, sharing some of Darija's rapid rhythm and consonant-cluster habits but with its own vocabulary, halba ("very/a lot") being a classic Libyan marker. Italian-derived loanwords, a legacy of colonial-era contact, show up where Moroccan or Algerian speech would reach for French instead. Eastern Libyan speech around Benghazi leans noticeably closer to Egyptian Arabic than western Libyan speech around Tripoli does.

clips.notes: 'Urban Hijazi' is specific enough to map confidently to the hejazi cluster, Jeddah being the canonical urban Hijazi center. Good candidate for the contrast pairing with clip06 (Najdi) for an 'expert mode' Hejazi vs Najdi minimal-pair round.
clips.reveal_draft: Urban Hijazi Arabic, the speech of Jeddah, Mecca, and Medina, sounds quite different from the Najdi Arabic of Riyadh despite both being "Saudi." Hejazi tends to keep /q/ as a uvular stop in many words rather than shifting it to /g/, and centuries as a pilgrimage and trade hub have given it a more cosmopolitan lexical mix than the more conservative, Bedouin-rooted dialects of the interior.

clips.notes: Najdi label maps cleanly to the najdi cluster. Long source video (timestamp 608s) — double-check the clip start lands cleanly on speech, not mid-word or over background music/intro.
clips.reveal_draft: Najdi Arabic, centered on Riyadh and the central plateau, is one of the more conservative dialects on the peninsula, retaining the interdental consonants th and dh that many other dialects have merged into /s//z/ or /d//t/. The classic giveaway is /q/ realized as /g/ (gahwa for "coffee"), paired with vocabulary like wayed ("a lot") that marks it as distinctly Bedouin-rooted rather than urban Hejazi.

clips.notes: Defaults to the algerian cluster (Algiers as representative city). If the speaker has Saharan/M'zab or Tuareg-influenced features (cf. Ghardaia/Tamanrasset notes in dialect-cities.json), reassign to 'saharanAlgerian' instead — same macro_group either way, so a near-miss still scores reasonably under two-tier scoring.
clips.reveal_draft: Algerian Darija shares the Maghrebi family's consonant-cluster tendencies and ma-...-sh negation with Moroccan Darija, but carries its own intonation and a lexical layer shaped by Algeria's particular history of French contact. If the speaker instead shows more Tuareg- or Saharan-influenced features, common further south toward Ghardaia or Tamanrasset, this would shift toward the Saharan Algerian end of the spectrum rather than the Algiers-coast variety tagged here.

clips.notes: 'Yemeni Arabic' is broad — Sanaani (north), Taizzi-Adeni (south/southwest), and Hadrami (east) are quite distinct. Defaulting to Sanaani as the most prestige/media-associated variety, but this needs a listen for region-specific lexical items and the characteristic Sanaani phonology before locking in the cluster.
clips.reveal_draft: Tagged here as Sanaani (Northern Yemeni) Arabic, one of three fairly distinct Yemeni dialect zones alongside Taizzi-Adeni in the south and Hadrami in the east. Sanaani is often described as unusually conservative, preserving features closer to Classical Arabic than most modern dialects, including retention of /q/ as a uvular stop. If the vocabulary or vowel patterns sound more "southern," this clip may actually belong to the Taizzi-Adeni or Hadrami clusters instead.

clips.notes: Lebanese label maps cleanly to the lebanese cluster. Beirut as default city is reasonable unless the speaker shows clear Beqaa/rural features (cf. Zahle note in dialect-cities.json), in which case the cluster is still correct but city should shift.
clips.reveal_draft: Lebanese Arabic, centered on Beirut, belongs to the broader Levantine family but has a sound of its own, most notably the imala effect, where a final -a in words like madrasa shifts toward an -e sound (madrase). Frequent code-switching with French and English is another strong marker, along with shu as the all-purpose "what." Rural Beqaa Valley speech around Zahle tends to carry somewhat more conservative features than Beirut city speech.

clips.notes: Dual label suggests the speaker may genuinely sit on the Jordanian urban / Palestinian urban continuum — these are mutually intelligible and share many features, so this could be a deliberate 'hard mode' edge case rather than a data error. Recommend keeping both jordanianU and palestinianU as acceptable answers (full macro_group credit either way) rather than forcing a single ground truth, and noting the ambiguity explicitly in the reveal text as a teaching moment.
clips.reveal_draft: This clip sits on the Jordanian-Palestinian dialect continuum, where urban varieties from Amman and from cities like Ramallah or Nablus are mutually intelligible and share most core features, including shu for "what" and kifak/kifik greetings. Because the two are so closely related, either is treated as correct for scoring here; a sharper cue would be specific vocabulary or place references that pin down which side of the Jordan River the speaker is from. If the speech instead shows kaf-to-tsh affrication, that points specifically to rural Palestinian fellahi speech, as in the Hebron area.
